speaker: interviewee
text: [TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It's the 12th of July, 1996. The survivor is Selien Abram. Interviewer is Mary Ziegler. We're in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. And the language this morning is English. Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's July the 12th, 1996. My name is Mary Ziegler. And I'm interviewing Selien Abram in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, we're going to to start with the formal part of the interview. Can you tell me your name and how to spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Selien, S-E-L-I-E-N. Abram, A-B-R-A-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Koperberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-P-E-R-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name-- when you were hiding, what was your name then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walsh.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: W-A-L-S-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first name then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lein.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: L-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, when's your-- what is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: 11th of September?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nearly 75. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your place of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the formal part done with. Selien, can you tell me who was in your family before the war when you were growing up? How big was your family? Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was an only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so your mother and father, what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Philip Koperberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was Miethe Prins.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were wholesalers in textile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he own his own business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a name, the business that he owned?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about Amsterdam, what it was like growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fantastic.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was a terrific city. And-- and it was a good life there, very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish life like in Amsterdam as a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very big. We were not only Jews. I never went to Jewish school or anything. But there was a big Jewish community. They killed 125,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you-- you weren't religious, so how did you identify as a Jew growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you knew you were Jewish. And sometimes, you had people who were nasty, like at one stage I had a girl at school who called me a bloody Jew. And I chased her. And it was Christmastime and she had plaits. And I was hanging on her plaits. I let my feet off the ground. And they called me the Christmas tree. [LAUGHS]. Honest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So look, there were always people who were anti-Semitic. But for the rest no, we never had any troubles. But did you identify as a Jew? Did you go-- did you observe the high holidays, things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The only thing we observed was Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had special dinners on Friday night. And if there were more people in the family, they came together. But that's about as far as it went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother light candles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, can you describe the home that you lived in with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was about three years old, we moved to a shop. We had a textile shop and a baby shop next door. And we lived behind the shop. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then when I was, oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the address of that shop? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the corner of [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the accommodation like behind the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two rooms, a lounge, a kitchen, and two bedrooms. That was it. I slept on the textile. And when I looked up at the cupboard, was textile, everywhere. [LAUGHS] That's how I grow up. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have things like a bathroom in that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not there. No, later we moved to a flat, when my father aspired to wholesale. And there we had a shower made in a cupboard, because there was none there. It was not in those days. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got married in '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that you went to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the-- which school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I first went to normal school. I don't know how you call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And the second school was an home science school by the name of fondet, D-E-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cooking, cleaning, science, dress-making, a bit of everything. I still have my report here. [LAUGHS] And I left that in '35.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I liked it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your favorite subjects?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my favorite subjects were reading and-- how do you call that? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] About-- about the country and-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Geography.

speaker: interviewee
text: Geography. And about other countries, that was very big in Holland, more than here. And I was quite good in that, so yeah, that was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your ambitions as a child going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to be a salesgirl, because I was brought [LAUGHS] up in business. But my mother didn't want that, so I became a dressmaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your mother. What sort of a woman was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very plain woman, very good little Yiddish mama. That's all. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a real businessman and outgoing tip. He liked to go out for dinner or for dancing or entertain people. Was a completely different type of man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have any interests besides his work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No no. His business, that was everything, the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it unusual to be an only child at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. My mother had a miscarriage when I was, oh, maybe three years old and that was very bad. And then she never wanted to have any more children, so I stayed on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the standard of living in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. My father always made good money. He was a hard worker. So I was very spoiled, because I was alone. And no, I was not short of anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for entertainment outside school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I went on bike rides. I went to gymnastics. I went in an stage club.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why I'm on stage now. [LAUGHTER] And so I had enough interests.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go on holidays at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much, because my mother always was in the shop, so she couldn't take me on holidays. Sometimes I went by myself, but that didn't work out very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- where would you go by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the beach. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selien, how did the Jewish population interact with the non-Jewish population generally in Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Dutch Jews were very assimilated. We mixed a lot. There was not such an gap when you are not and you are. That didn't-- didn't exist. And that saved my life too. So that was very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Hitler came to power in 1933--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and the following years, how did that impact on the-- on your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well my uncle was also a wholesaler in textile. That was in the whole family. And he went to Germany to do business. And there he saw the Hitlerjugend. And that was I think '38. And he came home distressed. He didn't get out of his hotel room. He was scared to go on the street. And-- and then a lot of immigrants came from Germany and Poland to Holland and told the stories. But we thought they exaggerate. We couldn't believe that that happened, those pogroms, until it hit us. And when Hitler came, the first year, year and a half, they didn't do anything. They leave-- let us alone. But there was a big mistake in Holland. You had to be registered. And there was an organization by the name of the Jews rat, Jewish council. And everybody gave the name. Stupid, but that's how it happened. And you realized that later, of course. And so when Hitler started, they know exactly where to go to pick up everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say they had to give their name, when was that already?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I think the beginning of '42, end of '41 when it started. First it was you had to give in your radio. Then it was everything what was copper or metal. And we did everything as long as they left us alone. But they didn't leave us alone. Then we had to wear the David Star.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know. Also about the same period. And that was disgusting. But what could you do? You couldn't do anything. Everybody said, as long as they leave us alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you, Hitler invaded Poland in '39.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that, when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did-- how did you and your family react to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. It didn't bother you that much, because it was far from your bed, so to speak. So you were not involved. And yes, as I said, we couldn't believe the stories what came out, until we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --were confronted with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet some of the refugees that came from Germany and Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And there was a special camp in Holland, by the name of Westerbork. It was in a-- like a [NON-ENGLISH]. And there, they interned those people. Some of them walked free, maybe so many or whatever, I don't know. But a lot of them were-- were in that camp. But it was not a nasty camp. They had little houses there. And they could-- they were practically free to go, so it was no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work at that time? Were you already working in '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: '39. Yeah. I was working.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dressmaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- who were you working for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, for firm. Big firm, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the firm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The first one was-- let me think. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS] Very funny name. And the second one was-- oh, I don't know. It was a German guy, but I can't remember his name. Little German man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was it working for a German man?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. No troubles. So by that time I finished my apprenticeship, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so I worked for him first as an apprentice. And then my teacher had to go in divorce courts to the Hague. And I was there on my own, so he said to me, you have to work for yourself now. I said, oh, that's fine. But when I worked for that woman, I made $2, 2 gilders 50, which was nothing. And then that day when she was not there, I made 1 and 1/2 [INAUDIBLE]. And then she came back. And I said, I'm not working for you anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a big upheaval, because that little girl there said she wasn't working anymore. So the boss didn't know what to do. And I said, if you don't let me work by myself, I'll leave. So she had no choice and I worked there. So that was that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I-- then I got married. I stopped, or maybe before I can't remember. So long ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about getting married? Who-- who was your fiance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heyman Waas. And we planned a big wedding, because I was an only child. And even so, we didn't go to synagogue. My father hired some people from the choir from the synagogue. And we hired a hall and music. Everything was set. But then they started to pick up boys. And everybody said it was too dangerous, because if so many men are together, they come in and pick all the boys out and take them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who picks up boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. And I got married I think the 3rd of July.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '41. And the 10 or something like that they started to pick up boys from the street. And we were in the city to buy something, that was my first husband and my second husband and his wife and their baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what was his-- what was their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Karel and--

speaker: interviewer
text: The baby was Karel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Karel Abram.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and the-- and his parents were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bluma Abram and Philip Abram. And we came out of the city and we were warned through Jewish people, go back, because in the next street they pick the men up. And we were desperate. We didn't know what to do. We didn't have a clue what was going on. And we hide somewhere. But the baby was in a pram, so we were stuck with the pram. We didn't have food for the baby. We didn't have nappies, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then my husband or what was then my friend said, across the street where they were picking up the boys, there is a guy I worked for, because he learned to be [INAUDIBLE] building. And he said, I'll go to those people when it's quiet and ask them if they'll keep the pram. And so we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we picked up a cab and went to my mother's-in-law place. And there we put towels on the baby, because we didn't have nappies. And I stayed 'til it was dark, and then they went home to their place and I went to my place. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and then I decided I'm not going to wait to be picked up. So I said, I'll go in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this together with your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my first husband, yeah. But he worked for a while for my father. And he had red hair, so from a mile away you could see him. And he worked in Limburg, that is south of Holland, of Amsterdam so to speak. And he went there, did some traveling. And people knew him, because he was a representative for the firm. And then when we fled, I went to a customer, but they were Jewish people too. And they had two boys. And she said, OK. I'll take you for a few days, but you can't stay here, because we don't know what happen to us. So we stayed there for a couple of days. In the meantime, my father made contact with somebody who lived in that area. And they were supposed to give us a hiding place. And I was three months pregnant for my son, my middle son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] then one day that woman walked in and said-- Which-- which woman walked in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was working for the underground movement.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I don't know. Can't remember. And then she said to us, I've got an address. But first, in lunchtime, you go to that and that street, that and that people, and stay there 'til the night. And in the nighttime, we will pick you up and bring you to a permanent address.

speaker: interviewer
text: So like I had an instinct that something was not OK. So I said to my husband, you walk one side of the street and I walk the other. And so we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we came to a corner and there were maybe six people from the village, what you could see real village people. And they were standing there talking. And I walked on the right side and he walked on the left. And they stopped him. But we were walking without a star, without the David Star, and no papers. We were not allowed to travel in a train or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they stopped him and asked him for his papers, which he didn't have. So they took him away. And I went to the people I was supposed to go to and I said, I'm going to give myself up and I'm not staying here and I [INAUDIBLE] myself in. And they said no, you've got a baby and you're not.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, half an hour later, the military police came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- the name of the town that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sittard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the people that you were with, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. The-- the-- the customer was-- I can't remember what the name was. Very nice people. I've got a photo in the kitchen from it. Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the military police took me away and brought me to the military place where they stayed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't know what to do. And in the night, I don't know how many times I called them, then I had an excuse, a bit of water, then I had to go to the toilet, any excuse to talk. But there was no talking. And then the next morning, they took us to another town, Maastricht.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you when they took you to another town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The military police and my husband, because they put him in another cell. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then we came in in Maastricht, and there was the jail. And he was put in the men's section and I was put in the woman's. And I stayed there for 12 days with two other people in a cell what was original for one woman, for one person. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your guards in the-- in Maastricht? Who were the-- were they Dutch or German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Dutch borders, yeah. And then after 12 days--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- can you tell me a bit about those 12 days? What-- did they give you food and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They gave you food. But you have to-- to live in a little space what actually was for one person.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you fellow prisoners in that cell?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was one lady who was caught at the border. She wanted to go over the border. And the other one was not Jewish at all, but married to a Jew. And they were musicians and they played in Maastricht music for a living. And somebody [INAUDIBLE] them in because he was Jewish. And she was brought in too. So we were there with the three of us. Then the 12 days, the warden came and said to me, you have to go to the white house. And the white house was where the SS was. But before it all started that I left Amsterdam and all that, I said to my husband, if we are caught, I'll take the blame. I'll say I'm pregnant and I'm nervous wreck and whatever. So we kept to that agreement. Then when I came outside, there was a policeman with a motorcycle with the side [INAUDIBLE], a sidecar. And I had to put up a star, a David Star. And there I went in the sidecar to [LAUGHS] the white house. And when I came there, there were three men there, three German. And they asked me I don't know how many questions, what I was doing and why I was there and all that business. And I should know I was not allowed to travel without papers and without a star and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] How do you feel? So I made all kind of excuses and they didn't say much. And then but when I went in that motorcycle, the policeman said to me, you must have regards for your husband. I said, how come? He said, he went this morning. And he want me to tell you that he's OK. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they drove me back. And when I came back in the cell, then they-- after maybe 5 or 10 minutes the warden came back. And she said, you can go. But I didn't believe it, because in those days, they said that to people to tease them and then they didn't let them go. So I was scared for that. And I said, are you having me on? No, you're free to go. But tonight you have to sleep with somebody from the Jewish Council. And they get you papers to travel. OK. So I slept with those people. Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No idea. Never saw him in my life. Anyway, then I came in Amsterdam. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get back to Amsterdam?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. I had special papers. And somebody warned my father that I was free. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, Selien, when was that? What-- what year and what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- I left in July.

speaker: interviewee
text: '42. And so I was 12 days there. And then when I came back, I didn't want to go in my own flat, because I didn't feel safe. But I had to go to a doctor to get a certificate that I was not able to look after myself and that I was pregnant and that I have to have permission to stay with my parents. So I got a letter from a doctor and I stayed with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop here, Selien.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of Tape 1 of the interview with Selien Abram. It's the 12th of July, 1996, and we are in Sydney, Australia.
